question: A pregnant patient is scheduled for cesarean delivery and undergoes preoperative ultrasound to assess bleeding risk. The imaging raises concern for suspected abnormal placentation with high risk of massive hemorrhage. Which operative management strategy is most appropriate?

options.A: Planned cesarean hysterectomy with blood products
options.B: Attempt placental removal with uterine repair
options.C: Trial of labor and vaginal delivery
options.D: Cesarean with intraoperative uterine artery ligation only

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the ultrasound image. I see a placenta-like echogenic layer along the uterine wall that contains multiple irregular hypoechoic/anechoic spaces within it and an area where the myometrial layer between the placenta and uterine serosa appears thin. These imaging features are consistent with an abnormally adherent or invasive placental implantation pattern on the uterine wall.

Next, I relate those findings to the clinical stem: the patient is scheduled for cesarean delivery and the imaging raises concern for abnormal placentation with high risk of massive hemorrhage. The question asks which operative strategy is most appropriate in this setting of suspected invasive placenta and high hemorrhage risk.

Then, I consider how each option aligns with that scenario. Option A (planned cesarean hysterectomy with blood products) provides definitive removal of the uterus containing the adherent placenta and anticipates significant bleeding by arranging blood products. Option B (attempt placental removal with uterine repair) would require manual removal of a likely adherent placenta and therefore risks provoking major hemorrhage. Option C (trial of labor and vaginal delivery) contradicts the planned cesarean and would expose the patient to uncontrolled bleeding risk. Option D (cesarean with uterine artery ligation only) offers a temporizing measure but may be insufficient if the placenta is deeply adherent or invasive.

Finally, given the ultrasound appearance suggesting invasive placentation and the stated high risk of massive hemorrhage, the safest, most definitive operative plan among the choices is a planned cesarean hysterectomy with preparation of blood products.
caption: Preoperative ultrasonography findings of a patient with anterior placenta previa indicating suspicious lacunar space, sponge-like findings in the cervix wall, and lack of retroplacental clear zone.
sub_caption: Preoperative ultrasonography findings of a patient with anterior placenta previa indicating suspicious lacunar space, sponge-like findings in the cervix wall, and lack of retroplacental clear zone.
image_context: After confirming the placenta location, US findings suggesting a risk of massive blood loss and placental adhesion should be checked. Many studies have reported sonographic findings predicting placenta accreta. Lacunae space (an irregular area of low echogenicity larger than 1 cm × 1 cm in the placental parenchyma) was strongly associated with placenta accreta [6,7]. Lacunae were graded according to their appearance and number, with Grades 2 and 3 at high risk for placental invasion. Grade 2 is defined as the presence of 4–6 lacunae that tend to be larger and more irregular in shape, while Grade 3 is characterized by many large and bizarrely-shaped lacunae throughout the placenta [8]. Sponge-like findings of the cervix wall (five or more hypoechogenic areas > 5 mm in diameter), lack of retroplacental clear zone (the clear zone; sonolucent zone between the placenta and myometrium), thinning (≤ 1 mm) of the myometrium, bridging vessels from the placenta to the uterine-bladder interface, and uterine-bladder interface thinning are also suggestive of abnormal placentation [6,7,9,10] (<xref rid="apm-20076f1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can also be used when US examination findings are inconclusive, particularly in women with abnormal placentation. In a systematic review and meta-analysis report, MRI was highly accurate for the detection of placental invasion presence, depth, and topography (). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can also be used when US examination findings are inconclusive, particularly in women with abnormal placentation. In a systematic review and meta-analysis report, MRI was highly accurate for the detection of placental invasion presence, depth, and topography (<xref rid="apm-20076f2" ref-type="fig">Fig. 2</xref>). Further, MRI and US showed similar performance in detecting the presence of invasive placentation [). Further, MRI and US showed similar performance in detecting the presence of invasive placentation [11]. However, the prenatal diagnosis accuracy using US exam may be insufficient, with a sensitivity of 0.53–0.85 and a specificity of 0.71–0.89 [10–12]. US alone has limitations for the prediction of bleeding in the intra- and postpartum periods. Therefore, the clinical characteristics should also be considered.

Lacunae were graded according to their appearance and number, with Grades 2 and 3 at high risk for placental invasion. Grade 2 is defined as the presence of 4–6 lacunae that tend to be larger and more irregular in shape, while Grade 3 is characterized by many large and bizarrely-shaped lacunae throughout the placenta. Sponge-like findings of the cervix wall (five or more hypoechogenic areas > 5 mm in diameter), lack of retroplacental clear zone (the clear zone; sonolucent zone between the placenta and myometrium), thinning (≤ 1 mm) of the myometrium, bridging vessels from the placenta to the uterine-bladder interface, and uterine-bladder interface thinning are also suggestive of abnormal placentation.
